I am a Nintendo fan from Saudi Arabia, and I would like to request Arabic language support for Nintendo Switch and Nintendo Switch 2 consoles, as well as Arabic localization for Nintendo first-party games.
The Arabic gaming community includes millions of players, and Saudi Arabia and the Gulf region

language support.
Adding Arabic to the system interface, menus, subtitles, and in-game text would significantly improve the experience for millions of players across the

services.

Sincerely,

Bandai Namco has been exemplary at marketing this game from beginning to end, again with the Switch/2 version and even though I was directly sold from day 1, I can't help but praise this marketing. That's how you market a game to get people interested. Many in the industry should take notes. 
Now even inside the same company some games aren't marketed as well as others but at least for the people who were in charge of marketing this game, I can definitely say they did a great job. That could be the start of a second wind for Digimon in the West. Now the Digimon team just has to double down on it with a next game to always put more eyes on Digimon.
Maybe not everyone will agree but I've always been fine with reusing assets from 1 game to another as long as the next game is as interesting and creative cause it makes releasing a new game so much faster. I don't think each game absolutely needs to be brand new in every way to be valuable and worth our time. The Legend of Zelda: Majora's Mask is a good example. With more games like Time Stranger Digimon will build a legacy of great games and that's how you make a name for your franchise.

Incredibly profound video game, wonderfully challenging (with an easier difficulty if that's not your beat). GOTY, don't care what some awards show says. It gets compared to both Hollow Knight and Sekiro a lot, but I think that maybe undersells it--it's less of a sprawling experience than either of them, but makes up for it in the writing, and as you can see here the art and music are gorgeous. In particular Yi, the player character, is a breath of fresh air compared to the typical silent protagonist--he's actually got much more knowledge of what's going on than the player initially does, and comes with his own opinions and biases like any person does (with some leeway for dialogue options of course). And the world they've built is fascinating, too.

To the uninitiated: This is NOT just a GB port of Donkey Kong Country! Some of the stage backdrops are based on DKC's (with entirely different level design), and then most of the environments are brand-new. Similarly, some of DKC's soundtrack was remixed, but the game also contains a ton of new tracks, which work very well on the Game Boy. Overall, not as good as any of the Countries, but a worthy and fascinating game in its own right.

I really miss the days when Just Dance made you move your whole body. I still remember playing it for the first time with Kinect. It was so much fun that even people who didn’t care about games got up and danced and it was exciting and fun that time. Now with the Joycon, all it takes is a flick of the wrist. It has lost the energy and it’s not even useful for fitness anymore. There’s barely any movement or challenge. The Switch 2 does have a camera, so the opportunity is there. But it’s really up to Ubisoft to bring back full-body motion detection. Without that Just Dance just doesn’t feel the same anymore. And i am in 20's and new modern song SUCKS so bring back the old.

What a wonderful game! I played this on my PS5 last year and it was my favourite game that year, the environments are beautifully designed the characters and story have a real depth to them and just make you want to know what happened to this world you're trapped in, and how it fell into a dystopian nightmare. It's not overly long in fact I'd say it's perfectly paced and doesn't outstay it's welcome, to the point that when it's done you feel utterly fulfilled. It's a very poignant story one that gripped me to the end and I genuinely felt real sadness along the way and especially at its climax. The music I have to give a big shout out to, it's magnificent and atmospheric, it's adds so much more to an already vibrant and enriched experiance so much so I bought the 2xLp gatefold vinyl, beautiful artwork to reflect a beautiful game.

Tetris Origins

I wish I could tell you how crazy my family and I were when we found out that this collection would include Tetris Battle Gaiden!
I still can't believe it: It feels like a dream. This game is so connected to my childhood.

The first time I was hit by the Princess's "Mirror", I was so shocked that I didn't know what was happening. The performance of the Shaman's special move "Curse", which my cousin liked, was very scary to us when we were still little. The sibling fights that broke out over Aladdin's "See-saw" and "Remote". My relatives who came to stay got hooked on it for some reason, and she were so happy when she finally beat Grand Princess after playing it late into the night. And I still remember that my grandfather, who didn't play games much, always used the Wolf Man to keep us company in this game. 
(Also, this was the first time we learned about Halloween in Japan, when Halloween was not yet common.) 

I'm really looking forward to playing this game with my family again.  And to be able to play with many other Tetris games. Thank you from the bottom of my heart!

Been playing it for awhile and the more I play of this game, the more I keep telling myself "Man, this should've been *primarily a CO-OP/2-PLAYER first* with the OPTION of playing it as singleplayer". In fact, not only would a co-op mode reduce the FRUSTRATION of controlling both characters at the same time (especially during dodging/countering), but also it would've led to so many *fun/memorable moments* if you're playing with your friend/sibling and manage to nail all combos/counters in-battle. Nintendo missed a BIG opportunity here IMO.

Don't forget that the concept of just listening to video game music is not a new thing; Nintendo just intentionally created a supply shortage of their own stuff over the years.  In the days of CD's, it was possible to get your hands on a Nintendo game OST legally, but the avenues were extremely limited.  Unless you had an option like happening to subscribe to Nintendo Power back when they still had their catalogs, you had to go to sites like Play Asia and have to deal with international shipping hassles.  Later, it was possible to dump those OST's for personal use like on an MP3 player, but even then, access through legal means was extremely difficult simply because Nintendo wouldn't contribute any options. First impressions after testing :
Pros:
-Ergonomic / Simple design
-Console music themes (only Wii for now, but it's cool to have them)
-Possibility to extends up to 1h a track and/or loop it.
-Legal way to listen to nintendo music

Cons:
-Some catalogues are incomplete (no Final lap version of MK8dx's tracks)
-Composers are not credited
-Huge lack of content

As it stands now, it's okay, but far from usable. Youtube already has all of those musics and more. But if it is updated regularly, I could see myself using it often whenever I'm in a 'Nintendo' mood.

2014 - Shulk gets added to Smash 
2015 - Xenoblade X and Xenoblade 3DS 
2017 - Xenoblade 2 
2018 - Torna: The Golden Country
2020 - Xenoblade DE
2021 - Pyra and Mythra added to Smash
2022 - Xenoblade 3
2023 - Future Redeemed 
2025 - Xenoblade X DE

Luigi's Mansion 3 is one of the best games on the platform both from a technical standpoint and outstanding animation you rarely see even in most AAA big budget blockbusters. That T-Rex skelleton boss fight in the museum has some crazy realtime animation apearing very lifelike in its movement. Can't help but give mad props to the talented devs for showing what an 8 year old outdated hardware is still capable of. Its nice seeing what a high end gaming rig can do but I would argue is even more impressive to see just how well a team can leverage a fixed closed system to deliver some magical moments despite its age

Mr. Tatsuke seems like a very nice guy. He alone is really tempting me to buy the game.

Worth mentioning, if anyone is worried that the game is too handholdy (like I was), you can individually turn off most if not all of the added QoL stuff, like the indicator that tells you when you will glimmer a skill, or even map markers.

This allows you to keep what you want and turn off what you think is too much. It's fantastic to have this level of customization over the game systems and I really appreciate this game for including it. It manages to cater both to hardcore SaGa fans as well as newcomers at the same time, with no compromise. Just amazing.

I am really excited for this remake. It looks and sounds gorgeous, and with the new content added, it's certain to be a big hit. I loved the original Romancing SaGa 2, and it's probably my favorite game in the SaGa series overall, so the potential for a remake like this had been on my mind since Minstrel Song came out on PlayStation 2 almost 20 years ago. A huge thank you to Tatsuke-san and all the staff who clearly gave their all for this project - it's a dream come true for many old fans like myself, and hopefully it will attract some new fans as well!

I played the remaster after hearing great things about the romancing saga series. I won't lie, the first time I played I dropped the game after 2 hours and picked it up again one year later and I was instantly hooked, the battles are hard and require you to use your full arsenal, while the game is open it always gives you instructions on what to do so you are never lost or wondering where to go next.
Many people consider this game to be a masterpiece and it's easy to see why, just don't expect the game to hold your hand because the game will definitely punish you (but I think it's hard to get into an impossible situation)

Xeen are one of the best dev studios out there. You guys absolutely killed it with Trials of Mana, and I was very impressed by the SaGa 2 demo - especially all the QoL updates and full voice acting.

I admit that I was never able to beat the previous remaster released for PS4, as the OG is just so damned hard. But I'm looking forward to this iteration so much and it's nice that you have the option to play on the classic difficulty - albeit with the new rest areas added to the dungeons which frankly is perfectly fine because I think the original was honestly too hard (it's the most difficult entry in the franchise IIRC and possibly the hardest RPG ever on the SNES)

I hope it sells well, and that SQEX continues to give you guys more projects ^^b
Thank you Tatsuke-san for all your efforts!
